risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The Principal Investigator used a computer-generated randomization schedule to randomize the participant... Randomization was conducted within three age strata (45–49, 50–54, and 55–70 years old) using the PLAN procedure of SAS (Cary, NC)." Comment: This explicitly describes a computer-generated random allocation sequence with stratification, which is a proper random method and supports low risk of selection bias for sequence generation.

Quote: "Following each pretest survey, the data collection interviewer emailed the Principal Investigator (SL) the contact information of the enrolled participant. The Principal Investigator used a computer-generated randomization schedule to randomize the participant and emailed the randomization assignment to the interventionist." Comment: Allocation was assigned by the principal investigator after enrollment and communicated to the interventionist, which indicates recruiters/interviewers did not have access to upcoming assignments before enrollment; although the report does not explicitly say 'concealed,' this centralized post-enrollment assignment process is sufficient to prevent foreknowledge for the enrolling staff.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Participants were randomized to a decision support intervention consisting of tailored telephone education about prostate cancer testing (experimental) or fruit and vegetable consumption (attention control)." and "Data collectors were blind to condition but the interventionists were not." Comment: Participants received different, clearly distinguishable telephone education topics and interventionists were explicitly unblinded. Because the main outcomes included self-reported knowledge, decisional conflict, intentions, physician discussion, and anxiety, knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence outcomes, so performance bias is high overall.

Quote: "Data collectors were blind to condition but the interventionists were not." Also: "Trained interviewers used a structured telephone interview to collect self-report data" and "medical claims data on PSA testing and physician visits were 100% complete"; "The report generator was blind to participants’ study condition." Comment: The outcome assessors for the self-report outcomes were blinded, and the objective claims-based outcomes were extracted/verified from medical claims with a blinded report generator. Because the main outcomes were either interviewer-collected self-report with blinded data collectors or objective claims data unlikely to be influenced by knowledge of allocation, detection bias is low.

Quote: "Attrition was low (12%) and did not vary by condition... Missing data at posttest were limited to self-reports of men lost to follow-up (N = 59/490 participants; 12%). There were no statistically significant differences between those who were and were not lost to follow-up on variables shown in Table 1 or on outcome variables assessed at pretest... it was not necessary to use imputation methods. We had complete data on PSA testing and physician visits via medical claims." Comment: Attrition was modest, balanced, and the authors report no baseline or pretest outcome differences between completers and non-completers; objective claims outcomes were complete. This makes attrition unlikely to have biased the results.
